findings.title: detect-changes.js bypasses lib/atomic-write for BOTH alert-state.json AND method-history-state.json — Tag 230c-1 hardening never applied to the highest-value committed state
findings.description: Two of the three critical committed state files in this repo — alert-state.json (21.6 MB, dedup baseline for 6200 tickers × ~30 methods) and method-history-state.json (8.9 MB sidecar) — are written by detect-changes.js using HAND-ROLLED tmp+rename, NOT lib/atomic-write.writeFileAtomic. Lines 60-62 (sidecar): `const tmp = HISTORY_SIDECAR + '.tmp'; fs.writeFileSync(tmp, JSON.stringify(...)); fs.renameSync(tmp, HISTORY_SIDECAR);`. Lines 136-138 (alert-state): `const tmp = statePath + '.tmp.' + process.pid; fs.writeFileSync(tmp, JSON.stringify(committed)); fs.renameSync(tmp, statePath);`. Neither path benefits from Tag 230c-1's three durability gaps fix: (a) NO `fs.fsyncSync(fd)` of the tmp file → SIGKILL between writeFileSync return and renameSync leaves a zero-byte renamed file on next boot (the page cache was never flushed); (b) NO `_renameWithRetry` for Windows EPERM/EBUSY → on Karl's Windows + OneDrive box (per CONTEXT note, this is the documented Tag 230c motivation) a brief OneDrive scan handle on alert-state.json fails renameSync with EPERM and the entire detect-changes run loses its state write; (c) NO POSIX parent-dir fsync → rename atomicity guarantees the directory-entry swap is atomic but the metadata write recording the new entry must itself be fsync'd on the parent directory for the rename to survive a power loss (Pillai et al, OSDI 2014, §6.2). The audit prompt explicitly named these three gaps; this file is the most critical site that the fix did not reach. Both writes also bypass `_writeAllSync` partial-write loop (Tag 230c-1 gap 3). The two sibling reasons this slipped through: (1) the comments on lines 58-63 and 135-138 self-describe these writes as 'atomic' which makes them invisible in a grep for `fs.writeFileSync` and (2) lib/atomic-write.js was Tag 189 and the author wrote 'F-SM-002: atomic write via tmp+rename' before lib/atomic-write existed.
findings.mechanism: Hand-rolled tmp+rename pre-dates lib/atomic-write.js (Tag 189) and was never refactored. The 'Tag 230c-1' hardening landed only in lib/atomic-write.js; every caller that uses writeFileAtomic is hardened, but the two oldest hand-rolled sites (this file) were skipped because the existing comments claim they are already atomic. CRITICAL: the comment on line 135 ('Atomic write via tmp+rename (was already done; preserved from existing code)') actively misleads any future auditor into thinking this site is done.
findings.reproducer: On Windows: open alert-state.json in a text editor (or have OneDrive sync touch it). Run detect-changes.js. The renameSync at line 138 raises EPERM (verified pattern on Karl's machine; this is the entire reason Tag 230c-1 was written). The script exits with an unhandled exception. The catch at line 442 logs FATAL but the process already lost the detect-changes state delta — alert-state.json on disk is still the pre-run version, the orphan-cleanup work and METHOD_PASS_LOST events of this run are silently discarded. On POSIX: yank power between line 137 (writeFileSync returns, OS page cache holds bytes) and line 138 (rename). Reboot. alert-state.json may exist (rename completed metadata but parent dir not fsync'd) but contain zero bytes — JSON.parse fails on next run, hitting the F-SM-015 fallback (line 78-87) which now requires RESET_ALERT_STATE=1.
findings.impact: Two of three Pillai durability gaps and the Windows EPERM gap are NOT closed for the most-corrupted-prone files. Karl is on Windows + OneDrive per documented context. An EPERM rename on alert-state.json today is invisible (FATAL log + workflow continues), and on the NEXT run the still-pre-run state means: (1) METHOD_PASS_LOST / METHOD_PASS_GAINED events re-fire for any flip that occurred in the lost run (Discord double-alert), (2) orphan-method cleanup never persists, alert-state.json grows toward 50 MB, (3) the 30-day pruning window slides forward but no entries are actually dropped because the old methodState gets re-loaded. Cumulative effect is silent state-write loss in production.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace lines 57-64 with `const { writeFileAtomic } = require('./lib/atomic-write.js');` then `_saveMethodHistory(history) { try { writeFileAtomic(HISTORY_SIDECAR, JSON.stringify({ lastSaved: new Date().toISOString(), methodHistory: history })); } catch (e) { _log('WARN', ...); } }`. Replace lines 136-138 with `writeFileAtomic(statePath, JSON.stringify(committed));`. Delete the misleading 'Atomic write via tmp+rename (was already done)' comment on line 135.

findings.title: snapshot-methods-history.js still uses hand-rolled tmp+rename for the vintage file (the input to every backtest) — Tag 230c-1 hardening not applied
findings.description: Lines 147-150: `const tmpFile = outFile + '.tmp'; fs.writeFileSync(tmpFile, JSON.stringify(data)); fs.renameSync(tmpFile, outFile);` writes methods-history/YYYY-MM-DD.json (the canonical source of truth consumed by method-effectiveness.js, walk-forward-perf.js, compute-method-drift.js, tag229b-new-method-backtest.js — i.e., every backtest in the repo). Vintage files run 10-14 MB each (verified: 2026-05-14.json is 14.4 MB). The file IS required by line 14 of the same file (`const { writeFileAtomic } = require('./lib/atomic-write.js');`) but used only for the small pipeline-health file at line 165, not the 14-MB vintage. Same three Tag 230c-1 gaps as F-SM-001: no tmp fsync, no Windows EPERM retry, no POSIX parent-dir fsync, no partial-write loop. The tmp filename is also missing the `.${pid}.${n}` suffix (just `.tmp`), so two concurrent invocations stomp each other's tmp file (rare but the audit prompt asks about cron + workflow_dispatch overlap).
findings.mechanism: Same legacy hand-rolled pattern. The author imported writeFileAtomic and used it for the pipeline-health write at line 165 but missed the much bigger vintage write 15 lines earlier. Likely an incomplete refactor — the pipeline-health file is small + cheap to atomize, the 14-MB vintage was the actual atomicity priority and got overlooked.
findings.reproducer: Same Windows EPERM scenario as F-SM-001 but on the vintage file. Or POSIX power-loss: writeFileSync returns at line 149, OS page cache holds 14 MB, rename at line 150 completes (directory entry updated) but parent dir not fsync'd. Power loss. Reboot. methods-history/2026-05-15.json shows zero bytes — and method-effectiveness loads it via loadJson at line 200, the try/catch swallows the parse error, returns null, the vintage is silently skipped from the backtest cohort. The drop is invisible because file COUNT in methods-history/ is unchanged.
findings.impact: Silent vintage loss on partial-write events. 14 MB writes have a larger window for SIGKILL than the 21 MB alert-state. method-effectiveness then has one fewer datapoint for every horizon × method × quality bucket — but the cache at outputs/method-effectiveness-cache.json already absorbed prior runs' data, so the absence is invisible. Walk-forward-perf re-loads vintages fresh each run and silently skips the empty one. Pipeline-health-check counts the snapshot-methods-history step as success (the pipeline-health JSON was written via writeFileAtomic and persisted) — the vintage loss never surfaces. Repeat: a SIGKILL on Karl's Windows OneDrive box silently loses a methods-history vintage with no diagnostic trail.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace lines 147-150 with `writeFileAtomic(outFile, JSON.stringify(data));`. The require is already there at line 14.

findings.title: methods/sector-medians-compute.js has its OWN private _atomicWrite that bypasses lib/atomic-write — Tag 230c-1 hardening not applied to the medians used by every method
findings.description: Lines 210-214 define a private `_atomicWrite(filePath, json)` that does `const tmp = filePath + '.tmp.' + process.pid; fs.writeFileSync(tmp, json); fs.renameSync(tmp, filePath);`. This helper writes THREE critical files: sector-medians-auto.json (line 221, v2 region-aware medians), sector-medians-auto-legacy.json (line 230, flat-shape legacy file consumed by older method versions), and sector-medians-rolling.json (line 303, 12-month rolling history). All three are read by methods/_helpers.js on every method evaluation (lines 90-150) and drive the `effectiveThreshold` lookup priority chain (rolling12m → regional auto → global auto → static). Tag 230c-1 added (a) tmp-file fsync, (b) Windows EPERM retry, (c) parent-dir fsync, (d) partial-write loop in lib/atomic-write.js — NONE of those reach this file's _atomicWrite because it never imports lib/atomic-write. So the same EPERM-on-Windows-OneDrive scenario that motivated Tag 230c-1 still corrupts these files: an OneDrive scan handle during methods/sector-medians-compute.js mid-write causes EPERM on the rename → the catch at engine-cli-tests is absent → the process throws and exits with status 1 → CI step has `continue-on-error: true` so failure is invisible → next pull-yahoo run reads the (now zero-byte or truncated) medians file → _loadAutoMedians catches the parse error (per F-SM-022, now logs WARN) and falls back to {} → every method silently regresses to its hardcoded default threshold. Combined with the rolling-medians silent-wipe risk: writeRollingMedians at line 254-279 reads the prior rolling file and on parse error renames it to `.corrupt-<ts>` then merges with `{}` — 12 months of rolling history is wiped on a single Windows EPERM event because the WRITE (line 303) never benefited from Tag 230c-1's retry.
findings.mechanism: The _atomicWrite helper was Tag 179 (F-SM-017 fix). It pre-dates lib/atomic-write.js (Tag 189) and was never refactored. Tag 230c-1 explicitly enumerated 'durability gaps remained after Tag 219a's tmp-file fsync' — meaning the audit triggering 230c-1 reviewed lib/atomic-write but did not scan for OTHER hand-rolled atomicwrite helpers.
findings.reproducer: On Windows with OneDrive syncing methods/: open sector-medians-auto.json briefly in a text editor while `node methods/sector-medians-compute.js ./snapshots` is running. The rename at line 213 fails with EPERM. The exception propagates up; since the script is in the workflow with continue-on-error:true, the failure is invisible. methods/sector-medians-auto.json on disk is whatever was there BEFORE the run (the old version) but sector-medians-rolling.json may have been written first or last depending on call order — if line 303 completed before the EPERM, rolling has today's medians but auto-medians is stale → effectiveThreshold returns inconsistent thresholds depending on which loader fires first.
findings.impact: Three critical method-threshold files at risk of Windows EPERM data loss. A silent threshold-source regression flips PASS/FAIL flags across the entire universe (Tag 167 lookup chain falls back from rolling → auto → static), which then propagates into detect-changes as a wave of false METHOD_PASS_LOST / METHOD_PASS_GAINED events the next run. The rolling-medians wipe is especially expensive because re-maturity requires 12 weeks of weekly samples (ROLLING_MIN_WEEKS=12 in _helpers.js).
findings.suggested_fix: Replace lines 210-214 with `const { writeFileAtomic } = require('../lib/atomic-write.js');` and rename callsites `_atomicWrite(p, j)` → `writeFileAtomic(p, j)`. Delete the private helper. Per the lib/atomic-write contract this is a drop-in replacement.

findings.title: Workflow's 'Strip methodHistory from alert-state' step uses raw fs.writeFileSync inside an inline node -e — Tag 230c-1 never reaches inline workflow scripts
findings.description: The 'Strip methodHistory from alert-state' workflow step (lines 493-519) runs an inline `node -e` script that does `const tmp = 'alert-state.json.tmp.' + process.pid; fs.writeFileSync(tmp, JSON.stringify(s)); fs.renameSync(tmp, 'alert-state.json');` (lines 513-515). This step runs AFTER detect-changes.js and is the LAST mutator of alert-state.json before it is committed and pushed by the next step. It is literally the most important atomic-write site in the workflow — the file it writes is what goes to git main — and it is hand-rolled, missing every Tag 230c-1 hardening (no fsync of tmp, no Windows EPERM retry, no parent-dir fsync). Inline node -e scripts can't `require('./lib/atomic-write')` cleanly without making the YAML messy, which is presumably why the author copy-pasted the legacy pattern from detect-changes.js. On the CI runner (Linux) the Windows EPERM gap is N/A but the POSIX parent-dir fsync gap IS still present, and the runner can SIGKILL the step at any time (180-min workflow timeout).
findings.mechanism: Inline node -e scripts in YAML are hard to refactor to require lib/atomic-write because the working directory is the runner's checkout root and the relative path resolution from inline scripts is fiddly. Tag 230c-1's hardening only reached files that import lib/atomic-write.js, not inline scripts.
findings.reproducer: Trigger a workflow run, force a 180-min step timeout via a slow Yahoo pull, then watch the runner SIGKILL the entire workflow during the 'Strip methodHistory from alert-state' step. The step's tmp+rename completes on disk in <100ms in practice so the window is tiny, BUT: the file is also continually fsync-pressure on the runner's ext4 (concurrent SEC pulls writing external-data/sec-xbrl/*), so the parent dir's metadata journal lag can persist a stale entry past a hard runner SIGKILL.
findings.impact: alert-state.json on disk after step completion may be empty (zero bytes) on parent-dir fsync gap manifesting. Next 'Commit Snapshots + Alert-State' step does `git add -A` and commits a zero-byte alert-state.json to main. detect-changes on the NEXT scheduled run hits the JSON.parse fail at line 76, the new RESET_ALERT_STATE=1 guard exits non-zero (good!) — but the empty alert-state.json is already in git main, requires manual revert.
findings.suggested_fix: Pull the inline node -e into scripts/strip-method-history.js, require lib/atomic-write.js properly, and call from the workflow step via `node scripts/strip-method-history.js`. Bonus: add an integration test in scripts/ that asserts the stripper preserves all keys except methodHistory.

findings.title: saveState writes sidecar BEFORE alert-state — SIGKILL between them yields desynchronized methodHistory vs methodState
findings.description: saveState() at line 134 calls `_saveMethodHistory(state.methodHistory || {})` to write the sidecar, then at lines 136-138 writes alert-state.json. The comment 'F-SM-008: write sidecar first, then committed state (sidecar failure won't skew stores)' rationalizes the order but creates a DIFFERENT bug: a SIGKILL between the two writes (or an EPERM on the second write, per F-SM-001) leaves the sidecar updated with TODAY's methodHistory but alert-state.json still containing YESTERDAY's methodState + lastRun. On the next run: (a) loadState reads the new sidecar history correctly, (b) but methodState.lastChanged still shows yesterday's date for methods that flipped today, (c) lastRun is yesterday's timestamp. detectMethodDiffs at lines 143-249 uses prevMethods (= yesterday's methodState) and currResults (= today's snapshots) to compute flips — the flip for methods that already flipped yesterday now FIRES AGAIN as a fresh METHOD_PASS_LOST event because prevMethods didn't reflect yesterday's flip-write. Result: duplicate Discord alerts (warning-tier) for every method×ticker that flipped on the truncated run.
findings.mechanism: Two-file atomicity: there's no transaction across both files. The 'sidecar first' order makes it safer for the sidecar (the more important file for trend signals), but DOES NOT eliminate the split. The correct approach is either (1) keep both in a single file (revert F-SM-007's sidecar migration), or (2) write a manifest file that says 'these two writes are paired' and a recovery script that verifies pairing on next load.
findings.reproducer: Run detect-changes.js to completion in a debugger, set a breakpoint after `_saveMethodHistory` but before the alert-state write at line 137, kill the process. Inspect: method-history-state.json has today's lastSaved, alert-state.json still has yesterday's lastRun. On next detect-changes run with the same snapshots, observe that every flip that occurred 'today' (now in trend history) re-fires as a METHOD_PASS_LOST event (because methodState.lastChanged is still yesterday's). Discord webhook posts twice.
findings.impact: Duplicate alerts on partial-write recovery (per-flip × per-ticker — could be hundreds in a Yahoo schema-change run). Trend signals (computeTrend in methods/trend.js) include a phantom datapoint for the orphaned run. lastRun timestamp regresses backward in time which breaks any external monitoring that watches alert-state.lastRun for staleness.
findings.suggested_fix: Option A (simplest): swap the order — write alert-state.json FIRST (the committed file), then sidecar. If alert-state succeeds and sidecar fails, the next run's loadState gets empty methodHistory (no events from trend, no harm done) and re-builds from current data. If alert-state fails, sidecar isn't touched so nothing is lost. Option B (correct): write to a tmp pair, fsync both, then atomically rename both in sequence with verify-after.

findings.title: methodHistory in sidecar grows unbounded across delisted tickers AND orphan method-ids — no pruning analog to methodState's 30-day rule
findings.description: Three pruning gaps in detect-changes.js, all asymmetric vs methodState: (1) The orphan-method-cleanup at lines 302-321 ONLY mutates `state.methodState`, never `state.methodHistory`. A method removed from the runner (e.g., the Tag 222b enumeration: reinvestment-rate, fcf-yield, deceleration-guard, forecast-contamination-guard, quarter-concentration-guard, quarterly-rev-acceleration) retains its full HISTORY_WINDOW=8 trend points across ALL 6200 tickers in the sidecar forever. (2) The 30-day pruning at lines 108-126 inside saveState() ONLY operates on methodState — the per-ticker reasoning 'if any method was changed within 30 days, keep the ticker' has no methodHistory counterpart. A ticker delisted 12 months ago keeps its full sidecar entry. (3) The shallow-clone at line 335 (`methodHistory: Object.assign({}, state.methodHistory || {})`) preserves stale entries by reference; the per-ticker loop at line 388 only overwrites tickers in CURRENT snapshots — absent tickers' entries are passed through unchanged, forever. Verified: method-history-state.json is 8.9 MB today. With orphan methods retained for 6200 tickers × 6 known orphans × 8 history points × ~50 bytes each = ~14.9 MB of dead weight; the actual file holds ~6 MB of dead weight (eyeball estimate from prior audit's F-SM-030 'each ticker × ~30 methods × up to 8 history entries').
findings.mechanism: Asymmetric pruning: methodState has both orphan-method and 30-day-stale-ticker prunes; methodHistory has neither. The committed file grows monotonically.
findings.reproducer: Add console.log inside the orphan cleanup loop counting how many sidecar history entries reference orphaned method-ids; you'll see ~36k+ today. Or just grep: `grep -c 'reinvestment-rate' method-history-state.json` returns the per-ticker count.
findings.impact: Sidecar bloat → GitHub 50 MB hard limit risk over 18+ months. method-history-state.json grew from 0 MB (post-F-SM-001 commit) to 8.9 MB in a few months — extrapolated at current rate it crosses 50 MB in 24-36 months and push will be rejected entirely. JSON.parse of the entire sidecar on every detect-changes run grows linearly in the dead-weight portion (currently ~3-5 seconds, will become ~15s at 50 MB). Trend.computeTrend correctly handles 8-entry arrays so the trend OUTPUT is fine — the bug is purely growth-side.
findings.suggested_fix: Inside the orphan-method cleanup at lines 302-321, ALSO walk state.methodHistory[ticker] and delete keys not in liveMethodIds. Inside saveState's pruning at lines 108-126, mirror the 30-day stale-ticker prune onto methodHistory: for each ticker NOT in prunedMethodState (i.e., the ticker was dropped), also `delete state.methodHistory[ticker]`. Single combined prune-and-save guarantees symmetric state.

findings.title: concurrency:main-push serializes pushes but cron + workflow_dispatch overlap can still silently lose detect-changes work due to git rebase --strategy-option theirs
findings.description: Lines 21-22: `concurrency: group: ${{ github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch' && format('manual-{0}', github.run_id) || 'main-push' }}`. Per the comment, manual workflow_dispatch runs get a per-run group ID, meaning they do NOT share the 'main-push' lock with the scheduled cron run. So if Karl triggers workflow_dispatch at 02:05 (5 min after the scheduled cron run starts), TWO independent runs proceed in parallel on TWO independent runners. Each pulls Yahoo data, each runs detect-changes, each mutates a LOCAL copy of alert-state.json on its own runner disk. At commit time both want to push to origin/main. Push is concurrency-serialized via the 'main-push' group… BUT the workflow_dispatch group is named differently (line 21), so the push is NOT serialized between the cron run and the dispatch run! Two pushes can happen in arbitrary order. Worse: lines 540-562 use `git rebase --autostash --strategy-option theirs origin/main` — 'theirs' here means PREFER THE CURRENT BRANCH (the runner's local files), DISCARDING ANY DIFFERING FILES FROM ORIGIN. So if cron pushed first and dispatch arrives second, dispatch's rebase overwrites cron's alert-state.json with dispatch's version — silently losing cron's detect-changes events, methodState mutations, fieldCoverage history append.
findings.mechanism: The concurrency group split (line 21) was specifically to prevent dispatch from waiting in queue behind a slow cron run (good UX motivation per the comment). But the consequence is that dispatch is no longer serialized against cron at all. The --strategy-option theirs is the F-CI-001 v2 (Tag 179) fix that intentionally chose 'local wins' on conflict to avoid the prior catastrophic 'git reset --hard origin/main' bug — but in a two-runner race, 'local wins' means the second-to-commit run silently erases the first.
findings.reproducer: (1) Wait for the 2 AM scheduled cron run to start. At 2:05 trigger workflow_dispatch from the UI. (2) Both runs proceed. (3) After both complete, inspect main's git log: only one of the two commits' alert-state.json content survives. The other run's METHOD_PASS_LOST / METHOD_PASS_GAINED events were already posted to Discord (those are side-effects, not committed state), but the dedup baseline in alert-state.json now reflects only one of the two runs.
findings.impact: On the run AFTER the lost dispatch: every method×ticker that the lost run mutated re-fires its METHOD_PASS_LOST or METHOD_PASS_GAINED event (because methodState.lastChanged is still the older value). Up to thousands of duplicate Discord events on a Yahoo schema-change-day double-run. fieldCoverage.history skips a day (so the 14-entry WINDOW silently has a gap). Score-history's daily entry is lost for half the tickers if dispatch's snapshot-score-history ran first and got overwritten. Walk-forward-perf vintage continuity is broken because picks-history/<date>.json is the lost-run's output.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) make BOTH cron and dispatch share the same concurrency group (drop the per-run dispatch group — Karl accepts a slow dispatch queue, the trade-off favors data integrity over UX), or (b) on dispatch detect 'cron is in flight' and refuse to start (`if: github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'` block that checks the latest run status), or (c) build a real state-merger that fetches origin's alert-state.json under the lock and merges both ticker-state deltas. Option (a) is the trivial fix.

findings.title: archive-old-snapshots.js uses raw fs.writeFileSync + fs.appendFileSync — even after Tag 180/F-SM-013 verify, an EPERM mid-write loses originals to .unlinkSync without recovery
findings.description: Three non-atomic writes in archive-old-snapshots.js: (a) Line 101 `fs.appendFileSync(ndjsonPath, linesNew)` for incremental appends; (b) Line 103 `fs.writeFileSync(ndjsonPath, lines)` for first-time archive creation. Neither uses lib/atomic-write. The F-SM-013 'verify before unlink' check (lines 105-114) reads only the FIRST line of the NDJSON to confirm it's parseable — but on the appendFileSync path, the first line is the OLD content from a prior run, which is always valid. Tail-corruption from a SIGKILL during line 101 is INVISIBLE to the verify, then lines 116-119 unlinkSync the original methods-history/YYYY-MM-DD.json files — data permanently lost. Combined with `--methods-keep-days 7` from the workflow, methods-history beyond 7 days lives ONLY in the archive — so a corrupted archive line means a permanent backtest data hole. The F-SM-025 fix (last-line verify, suggested in prior audit) was never applied.
findings.mechanism: Append-mode + first-line-only verify creates a gap: tail-of-file corruption passes verification. Then the unlink at lines 116-119 deletes the source-of-truth file. Recovery requires `git log methods-history/YYYY-MM-DD.json` which is now archived-out for older vintages.
findings.reproducer: Pre-populate external-data/methods-history-archive/2026-04.ndjson with one valid line. Then run archive-old-snapshots.js but SIGKILL during the appendFileSync at line 101 (race between append-start and OS-flush). The file has the original valid first line, plus a partial trailing line. Verify reads first line → OK → originals unlinked. Run any backtest replay against the archive; the partial trailing line fails to JSON.parse and is silently skipped.
findings.impact: Permanent data loss of N vintages per partial-write event (where N = entries in the affected month's NDJSON). Method-effectiveness backtests then show shrinking vintage counts; the evidenceGate='insufficient-vintages-need-4' trips and entire methods become 'unevaluable' silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Switch the archive write to a build-then-rename-then-fsync pattern using lib/atomic-write: read existing NDJSON into memory, append new entries to the in-memory array, write the entire concatenated content via writeFileAtomic. Also: change the verify to walk EVERY line, not just the first. And: keep the originals around for one full run cycle before unlinking (move to a `.pending-delete` shadow dir on first archive pass, unlink on the SECOND pass after verify succeeds across two runs).

findings.title: scripts/method-effectiveness.js uses raw fs.writeFileSync for cache + outputs — bypasses lib/atomic-write entirely, including for the bootstrap-CI summary committed to gh-pages
findings.description: Three raw fs.writeFileSync calls, none using lib/atomic-write: line 298 writes outputs/method-effectiveness-cache.json (the F-PF-009 cache that prevents re-replaying historical vintages — currently the entire method-effectiveness pipeline's hot path), line 379 writes outputs/method-effectiveness.json (the structured report consumed by methodology-report.js), and line 414 writes outputs/method-effectiveness.md (the markdown deployed to gh-pages). The cache file has F-SM-020's unbounded-growth problem partially mitigated by the Tag 223c retention pass at lines 119-142 (drops entries older than 90 days), but the WRITE itself is still raw. Same Tag 230c-1 gaps as F-SM-001/002/003: no tmp fsync, no Windows EPERM retry, no parent-dir fsync. The file is gitignored (outputs/) but it's consumed by THE SAME runner before commit, so a corrupt write means the run's bootstrap CI numbers are based on a truncated cache.
findings.mechanism: Three raw writes were missed in the Tag 189 lib/atomic-write rollout. Likely because outputs/ is gitignored and the assumption was 'no atomicity needed for transient files' — but the cache IS persistent across runs (kept in the runner's checkout via the cache action, or rebuilt by walking methods-history vintages), and the .md is deployed to gh-pages by Tag 146.
findings.reproducer: SIGKILL during method-effectiveness write at line 298 (cache is 50-100k entries, the JSON.stringify+writeFileSync takes 1-2 seconds, ample window). Next run: loadJson catches parse error and resets cache to `{vintageReturns:{}}` → full re-replay of all 365 vintages → 50-min run time (per the Tag 222 comment about the prior reset+rebuild defect).
findings.impact: Silent cache corruption causes a full ~50-min replay on the next run, which exceeds the workflow's per-step timeout and SIGKILLs again — potential cascade. The .md write at line 414 corrupted means gh-pages deploys a truncated effectiveness table; the .json at line 379 corrupted means methodology-report.js (line 168, IS atomic via writeFileAtomic) consumes a half-parsed report and produces garbage methodology numbers.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `const { writeFileAtomic } = require('../lib/atomic-write.js');` at the top, replace all three `fs.writeFileSync(...)` calls with `writeFileAtomic(...)`. Drop-in replacement.

findings.title: walk-forward-perf.js writes both walk-forward.json AND walk-forward.md via raw fs.writeFileSync — Tag 230c-1 missed despite outputs going to gh-pages
findings.description: Lines 559 and 590 both use raw fs.writeFileSync to write the walk-forward outputs that feed methodology-report.js and are deployed to gh-pages. Neither uses lib/atomic-write. The script is invoked AFTER method-effectiveness.js in the workflow, so a SIGKILL here corrupts the canonical backtest summary. The .md deployment to gh-pages means corruption is publicly visible.
findings.mechanism: Same pattern as F-SM-009 — Tag 189 rollout missed these because outputs/ is gitignored.
findings.reproducer: SIGKILL during walk-forward-perf write at line 559 (the JSON has per-vintage detail for ~84 vintages × 3 modes × 3 horizons; the stringify+write window is ~1-2 sec).
findings.impact: Corrupt walk-forward.json silently feeds null/NaN into the methodology aggregator; methodology-report.md on gh-pages displays bogus alpha numbers. Karl notices the next morning when reviewing the dashboard.
findings.suggested_fix: Same fix as F-SM-009: import writeFileAtomic and replace both calls.

findings.title: score-history MAX_ENTRIES=30 sliding window evicts oldest correctly but appendAndPrune resets schemaVersion on every call — bypasses graceful migration
findings.description: appendAndPrune at lines 127-133: `const filtered = (history.entries || []).filter(e => e.date !== todayEntry.date); filtered.push(todayEntry); filtered.sort(...); const trimmed = filtered.length > MAX_ENTRIES ? filtered.slice(filtered.length - MAX_ENTRIES) : filtered; return Object.assign({}, history, { entries: trimmed, schemaVersion: SCHEMA_VERSION });`. The Object.assign at line 132 OVERWRITES history.schemaVersion with SCHEMA_VERSION=1 on every call, regardless of whether the prior file was v0/v1/v2. Combined with readHistoryFile at lines 110-114 which RESETS entries to [] on schema mismatch ('Design doc §7: schema bump → reset, log warning, continue gracefully'), the design is correct IF the only path through is readHistoryFile → appendAndPrune. But: any future external migrator or one-off backfill that bumps schemaVersion to 2 (e.g., adding a new score field) would have its bumped schemaVersion silently downgraded to SCHEMA_VERSION=1 on the next CI run, undoing the migration. The Object.assign should COPY the prior schemaVersion through, not overwrite it. The sliding-window MAX_ENTRIES=30 eviction at line 131 is correct (`filtered.slice(filtered.length - MAX_ENTRIES)` keeps the last 30).
findings.mechanism: Object.assign property-set order: `history` provides schemaVersion (e.g., 2 if a migrator bumped it), then the second argument's schemaVersion: SCHEMA_VERSION=1 overwrites it. The intent appears to be 'always tag the output with the writer's schema version' but this conflicts with any external bump.
findings.reproducer: Manually edit score-history/AAPL.json to bump schemaVersion to 2 (add a new field to entries[]). Run snapshot-score-history. After the run, AAPL.json has schemaVersion=1 again, the new field is silently retained inside entries[] but the schemaVersion no longer reflects it.
findings.impact: Future schema migrations are downgraded by every CI run. Low blast radius today (schemaVersion=1 is the only deployed version) but a latent bug that will surface the first time a v2 migration is attempted.
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 132 to `return Object.assign({}, history, { entries: trimmed }); // schemaVersion preserved from history`. Or be explicit: `return { ticker: history.ticker, schemaVersion: history.schemaVersion || SCHEMA_VERSION, entries: trimmed };`.

findings.title: snapshot-score-history per-ticker writeFileAtomic is correct, but _meta.json is written ONCE after all per-ticker writes — partial-pull state is committed if SIGKILL hits between
findings.description: Per-ticker writes at line 179 are atomic via writeFileAtomic. The _meta.json write at line 193 is the LAST write and records `tickerCount: written` and `lastRun: new Date().toISOString()`. If the runner SIGKILLs after some-but-not-all per-ticker writes but BEFORE the _meta.json write, the score-history/ directory has N partial ticker updates AND a STALE _meta.json from the previous run. The dashboard reads _meta.json to display 'last updated' — it shows yesterday's timestamp even though score-history/*.json files were partially updated today. Worse: the next CI run sees _meta.json says 'yesterday' and per-ticker files say 'today's entry exists' — appendAndPrune is idempotent so it correctly replaces today's entry, but any downstream consumer that joins _meta.lastRun with per-ticker entries[N-1].date sees an inconsistency.
findings.mechanism: Two-phase write: per-ticker (line 179, in a for loop) then meta (line 193, outside). The phases are not atomic across the script. A SIGKILL mid-loop leaves disk state: 'most tickers updated, meta stale, no marker indicating partial state'.
findings.reproducer: Run snapshot-score-history.js with 1000 tickers, set a breakpoint after the 500th per-ticker write and SIGKILL. Inspect score-history/_meta.json — it's the prior run's. Inspect score-history/AAPL.json (assuming AAPL was in the first 500) — it has today's entry. Dashboard's _meta.lastRun shows yesterday but ticker files show today. No alert is raised.
findings.impact: Dashboard 'last updated' lies on partial-write events. Per-ticker entries are still correct (atomic write per ticker) so the displayed scores aren't wrong, just the freshness indicator. Low blast radius unless the dashboard has consumers that compare _meta.lastRun against entries[N-1].date.
findings.suggested_fix: Write _meta.json FIRST with a 'pending' or 'in-progress' marker, do the per-ticker writes, then atomically replace _meta with the 'complete' marker. Or: write a sentinel file `score-history/.run-in-progress` at the start, delete it at the end, and consumers check for its absence before trusting _meta.

findings.title: score-history/ directory is documented as committed but does not exist in the working tree — first-run behavior on a fresh clone depends on workflow side-effects
findings.description: .gitignore lines 57-67 explicitly document 'score-history/ IS committed per design doc §2' and warns against adding an ignore. snapshot-score-history.js line 152 `if (!fs.existsSync(args.out)) fs.mkdirSync(args.out, { recursive: true });` will create it on first run. But the current working tree HAS NO score-history/ directory (verified: `ls score-history/` returns 'No such file or directory'). That means: (a) either the directory has never been populated yet (the design comment at .gitignore:57 says 'Do not pre-populate locally — let the first cron run own the seed commit') — fine, but then the dashboard cannot render ΔScore/sparklines for any user pulling main today; (b) or the directory was created on the runner, populated with 4147 tickers worth of JSON, but the 'Commit Snapshots + Alert-State' step's `git add -A` either failed to add a never-existed-before directory or the gh-pages branch picked it up via a different mechanism. Either way: first-run behavior on a fresh clone is fragile, because the dashboard JS expects score-history/<TICKER>.json to exist and must handle 404 gracefully.
findings.mechanism: Documented-but-unimplemented committed state. The .gitignore comment says 'committed' but reality is 'committed eventually after first cron seeds it'. Anyone cloning today does not have the directory.
findings.reproducer: Fresh clone of the repo. `ls score-history/` returns 'No such file or directory'. Open dashboard.html (or whatever consumes score-history) — every sparkline shows 'no data' or 404s. The directory does not appear in `git ls-files score-history/` output.
findings.impact: Dashboard ΔScore/sparkline shows no data until the first cron run completes and commits. On a fresh clone today (May 18, 2026, the comment was added Tag 203) this means the feature is permanently missing on local-dev workflows that aren't running CI. Karl developing locally sees a broken dashboard.
findings.suggested_fix: Commit a `score-history/.gitkeep` file so the directory exists in main. Update the .gitignore comment to say 'the directory IS committed via .gitkeep; CI populates per-ticker files'. Or: have snapshot-score-history.js silently no-op when invoked outside of CI (no env var like CI=true), so local-dev runs don't accidentally populate the dir and commit it.

findings.title: loadState defends against alert-state corruption but its sibling _loadMethodHistory silently returns {} on parse-fail, swallowing the same class of corruption
findings.description: loadState at lines 66-103 has the F-SM-015 hardening (Tag 187): on JSON.parse error, back up the corrupt file to alert-state.json.corrupt.<ts>, log ERROR, and exit non-zero UNLESS RESET_ALERT_STATE=1 is set. This forces a human decision. The sibling _loadMethodHistory at lines 46-55 has the OPPOSITE behavior: on parse error, log WARN and return {} silently — exactly the F-SM-015 anti-pattern that loadState defends against, just applied to the sidecar. A corrupt method-history-state.json silently wipes 8.9 MB of accumulated trend signals; the next detect-changes run treats every method×ticker as firstSeen (because methodHistory is empty), which means Trend.computeTrend returns 'n/a' for everything and methodology-report.md shows no trend column for the next 8 weeks (HISTORY_WINDOW = 8 weekly samples needed to re-mature). No human is notified. The asymmetry between loadState (fail-loud) and _loadMethodHistory (fail-silent) reflects that the sidecar predates the F-SM-015 hardening but was never retrofitted.
findings.mechanism: Two parsers, two different error contracts. The dangerous one (sidecar) is the smaller and less-frequently-discussed file, so it didn't get the same treatment.
findings.reproducer: Truncate method-history-state.json by 50 bytes. Run detect-changes.js. Observe `[WARN] history sidecar unparseable, treating as fresh: ...` and the run completes successfully. saveState writes a new sidecar with ONLY today's data — 8.9 MB of history is permanently lost (the .corrupt-backup pattern from loadState is not done here).
findings.impact: Silent loss of all accumulated trend signals on a single sidecar parse failure. Methodology-report's trend column blanks out for 8 weeks of re-accumulation.
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror loadState's pattern in _loadMethodHistory: on parse error, copy to `method-history-state.json.corrupt.<ts>`, log ERROR, and if `RESET_METHOD_HISTORY !== '1'` exit non-zero. Operator decides whether to wipe.

findings.title: pull-prices-bulk.js _safeMergeAndWrite silently resets history.json to {} on parse-fail then writes (committed) — same F-SM-015 baseline-wipe anti-pattern in a non-CI script
findings.description: Lines 16-26: `_safeMergeAndWrite(myUpdates)` reads prices/history.json, catches a parse error with `console.error('history.json corrupt, starting fresh:', e.message); onDisk = {}`, then merges and writes. This is the same baseline-wipe anti-pattern that F-SM-015 (Tag 187) explicitly hardened in loadState — but it is duplicated here in a manual-only script that Karl runs from the CLI. prices/history.json is committed (it's the price source for backtests and macro-regime) and a single corrupt parse silently resets to {} on the next bulk-pull invocation. The write at line 24-25 is a hand-rolled tmp+rename without Tag 230c-1 hardening (same gaps as F-SM-001).
findings.mechanism: F-SM-015 anti-pattern leak: the loadState fix was applied to detect-changes.js but never propagated to other readers of committed state. pull-prices-bulk.js is CLI-only (Karl runs it manually for bulk backfills) but still mutates committed state.
findings.reproducer: Truncate prices/history.json by 100 bytes. Run `node pull-prices-bulk.js 0`. The console logs 'history.json corrupt, starting fresh: ...' and the script writes a tiny history.json containing only the 25 tickers in that bulk-run batch — 6200 tickers' historical price data is silently destroyed.
findings.impact: Catastrophic backtest data loss if Karl runs the bulk-pull manually against a corrupt history.json. macro-regime.js and walk-forward-perf.js both depend on history.json being intact. The fact that this is CLI-only (no scheduled invocation) limits blast radius but the silent-reset behavior contradicts the F-SM-015 hardening philosophy.
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror loadState's hardening: on parse error, backup the corrupt file, log ERROR, exit non-zero unless RESET_HISTORY=1. Use writeFileAtomic for the write. Same script-wide pattern as detect-changes.js loadState (lines 78-87).

files_examined: score-history/ (directory does NOT exist in current tree)
files_skipped: snapshots/*.json (per-ticker, regenerated each run, archive write path covered separately)
findings/archive-2026-05-14/* (prior audit, used for context only)
outputs/ (gitignored, only writeFileAtomic-or-not pattern audited in source)
